questions.prompt: Read: "Mum, I'm bored." "OK, ___ take you to the park, then." The parent decides at that moment. Which reply fits?

options.text: I'm going to
I'll
I going to
I will to

questions.explanation: The parent reacts on the spot, so it's a will decision: I'll take you to the park. "I'm going to" implies a plan made before the child spoke, "I going to" drops the be (am), and "I will to" adds a wrong to after will.

options.text: Look — that glass is going to fall!
I promise I won't be late.
Are they going to sell the house?
She will going to start university in September.

questions.explanation: The error is (d): you can't combine will and going to — say "She is going to start" or "She will start". (a) is an evidence prediction, (b) a promise with won't, and (c) a correct going-to question — all fine.

questions.prompt: Read: "We've booked the tickets and packed our bags. We leave on Saturday." Which sentence best describes the trip?

options.text: They are going to travel on Saturday.
They will maybe travel one day.
They aren't going to travel.
They will going to travel on Saturday.

questions.explanation: Booked tickets and packed bags show a firm plan decided in advance, so use be going to: They are going to travel on Saturday. (b) sounds vague and uncertain, (c) contradicts the text, and (d) wrongly stacks will and going to.

questions.prompt: Complete the pair: "___ you ___ apply for the job?" — "Yes, I sent the form yesterday."

options.text: Will / to
Do / going to
Are / going to
Is / going to

questions.explanation: The answer ("I sent the form yesterday") shows an existing plan, so the question is a going-to one, inverting be with you: Are you going to apply? "Is" is for he/she/it, "Do" is the wrong helper, and "Will / to" both misuses will and adds an extra to.

options.text: I think it going to be a great match, and our team will to win.
I think it's going to be a great match, and our team will win.
I think it will to be a great match, and our team is going win.
I think it be going to a great match, and our team will winning.

questions.explanation: Only (b) is correct: an evidence/feeling prediction (it's going to be) plus an opinion (our team will win), both with the base verb. (a) drops the be and adds "will to", (c) adds "will to" and drops to in "going win", and (d) breaks the form and uses "will winning".

questions.prompt: Read: "Ahmed studied medicine for six years. He has just finished his exams and accepted a job at the city hospital. He starts next month." Which statement is TRUE?

options.text: He is going to stop studying and travel the world.
He will probably never work in a hospital.
He is going to begin working at the hospital next month.
He is going to retake his exams.

questions.explanation: He accepted a hospital job and "starts next month", so the planned future is: He is going to begin working at the hospital next month. (a), (b) and (d) all contradict the text — he finished his exams and took the job, so he isn't retaking exams, avoiding hospitals, or off to travel.
